{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{For|the Buddhist temples in [[Chiba Prefecture]] and [[Shimane Prefecture]]|Seichō-ji|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kiyomizu-dera | native_name = 清水寺 | image = Kiyomizu.jpg | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kiyomizu-dera is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|42|N|135|47|06|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | sect = Kita-Hossō | deity = [[Kannon]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|www.kiyomizudera.or.jp/en}} | founded_by = [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], reconstructed by Tokugawa Iemitsu | established = 778 | year_completed = 1633 (reconstruction) }} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Kiyomizu-dera (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = "Kiyomizu-dera" in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.45 | kanji = 清水寺 | hiragana = きよみずでら | romaji = Kiyomizu-dera }}
{{Nihongo|lead=yes|'''Kiyomizu-dera'''|清水寺||{{lit|Pure Water Monastery}}}} is a [[Buddhist]] temple located in eastern [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The temple is part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/688/multiple=1&unique_number=814|title=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|access-date=2008-12-20}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Circa 1880 detail, from- Adolfo Farsari - Kiyomizu Kyoto (cropped).jpg|thumb|Kiyomizu-dera circa 1880 by [[Adolfo Farsari]]]] The temple was established in 778, during the late [[Nara period]], by [[Enchin Shonin]], who was a priest from [[Nara (city)|Nara]] (the capital of Japan from 710 to 784). He is said to have received a vision telling him to construct the temple next to the Otowa spring.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kiyomizu-dera |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Kiyomizu-dera/ |access-date=2022-07-11 |website=World History Encyclopedia |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":1" />
In 798, the [[shogun]] [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue Tamuramaro]] improved the site by including a large hall that was reassembled from the palace of [[Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kammu]] (r. 781–806). The emperor had left Nara to escape the strong influence that the Buddhist monasteries had on the government there. During this period there was a strong rivalry between the [[Kōfuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]] and the Kiyomizu-dera temples, and both had influence around the region.<ref name=":0" />
Many of the temple's present buildings were constructed in 1633 on the orders of the shogun [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]].<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=37}}</ref> There is [[Japanese carpentry|not a single nail used]] in the entire structure. It takes its name from the waterfall within the complex, which runs off the nearby hills. ''Kiyomizu'' means "pure water".<ref name="PacificFriend">{{cite web|url=http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|title=Kiyomizu Temple|access-date=2008-12-18|date=2007-04-07|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090403175600/http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|archive-date=2009-04-03}}</ref><ref name="SacredDestinations" />
It was originally affiliated with the old and influential [[Hossō]] sect, dating from Nara times.<ref name=":1">Graham (2007), p. 32</ref> However, in 1965 it severed that affiliation, and its present custodians call themselves members of their own Kita-Hossō (Northern Hossō) sect.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kiyomizu-deploy |url=http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230712193733/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2023-07-12}}</ref>
=== Present ===
The expression "to jump off the stage at Kiyomizu" is the Japanese equivalent of the [[English language|English]] expression "to take the plunge".<ref name="SacredDestinations">{{cite web |title=Kiyomizudera, Kyoto |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912172631/http://www.sacred-destinations.com:80/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2009-09-12 |access-date=2008-12-18}}</ref> This refers to an [[Edo-period]] tradition that held that if one survived a {{convert|13|m|ft|adj=on|sp=us|abbr=off}} jump from the stage, one's wish would be granted. During the Edo period, 234 jumps were recorded, and of the jumpers, 85.4 per cent survived.<ref name="SacredDestinations" /> The practice was prohibited in 1872.<ref name="SacredDestinations" />
The temple complex includes several other shrines, among them the Jishu Shrine, dedicated to [[Ōkuninushi]], a god of love and "good matches".<ref name="PacificFriend" /> Jishu Shrine possesses a pair of "love stones" placed {{convert|10|m|ft|sp=us|abbr=off|-1}} apart,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jishujinja.or.jp/koiuranai/ |title=恋占いの石 |access-date=2020-08-03}}</ref> which lonely visitors can try to walk between with their eyes closed. Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>

It was originally affiliated with the old and influential [[Hossō]] sect, dating from Nara times.<ref name=":1">Graham (2007), p. 32</ref> However, in 1965 it severed that affiliation, and its present custodians call themselves members of their own Kita-Hossō (Northern Hossō) sect.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kiyomizu-deploy |url=http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230712193733/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2023-07-12}}</ref>
=== Present ===
The expression "to jump off the stage at Kiyomizu" is the Japanese equivalent of the [[English language|English]] expression "to take the plunge".<ref name="SacredDestinations">{{cite web |title=Kiyomizudera, Kyoto |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912172631/http://www.sacred-destinations.com:80/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2009-09-12 |access-date=2008-12-18}}</ref> This refers to an [[Edo-period]] tradition that held that if one survived a {{convert|13|m|ft|adj=on|sp=us|abbr=off}} jump from the stage, one's wish would be granted. During the Edo period, 234 jumps were recorded, and of the jumpers, 85.4 per cent survived.<ref name="SacredDestinations" /> The practice was prohibited in 1872.<ref name="SacredDestinations" />
The temple complex includes several other shrines, among them the Jishu Shrine, dedicated to [[Ōkuninushi]], a god of love and "good matches".<ref name="PacificFriend" /> Jishu Shrine possesses a pair of "love stones" placed {{convert|10|m|ft|sp=us|abbr=off|-1}} apart,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jishujinja.or.jp/koiuranai/ |title=恋占いの石 |access-date=2020-08-03}}</ref> which lonely visitors can try to walk between with their eyes closed. Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{For|the Buddhist temples in [[Chiba Prefecture]] and [[Shimane Prefecture]]|Seichō-ji|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kiyomizu-dera | native_name = 清水寺 | image = Kiyomizu.jpg | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kiyomizu-dera is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|42|N|135|47|06|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | sect = Kita-Hossō | deity = [[Kannon]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|www.kiyomizudera.or.jp/en}} | founded_by = [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], reconstructed by Tokugawa Iemitsu | established = 778 | year_completed = 1633 (reconstruction) }} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Kiyomizu-dera (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = "Kiyomizu-dera" in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.45 | kanji = 清水寺 | hiragana = きよみずでら | romaji = Kiyomizu-dera }}
{{Nihongo|lead=yes|'''Kiyomizu-dera'''|清水寺||{{lit|Pure Water Monastery}}}} is a [[Buddhist]] temple located in eastern [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The temple is part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/688/multiple=1&unique_number=814|title=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|access-date=2008-12-20}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Circa 1880 detail, from- Adolfo Farsari - Kiyomizu Kyoto (cropped).jpg|thumb|Kiyomizu-dera circa 1880 by [[Adolfo Farsari]]]] The temple was established in 778, during the late [[Nara period]], by [[Enchin Shonin]], who was a priest from [[Nara (city)|Nara]] (the capital of Japan from 710 to 784). He is said to have received a vision telling him to construct the temple next to the Otowa spring.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kiyomizu-dera |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Kiyomizu-dera/ |access-date=2022-07-11 |website=World History Encyclopedia |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":1" />
In 798, the [[shogun]] [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue Tamuramaro]] improved the site by including a large hall that was reassembled from the palace of [[Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kammu]] (r. 781–806). The emperor had left Nara to escape the strong influence that the Buddhist monasteries had on the government there. During this period there was a strong rivalry between the [[Kōfuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]] and the Kiyomizu-dera temples, and both had influence around the region.<ref name=":0" />
Many of the temple's present buildings were constructed in 1633 on the orders of the shogun [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]].<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=37}}</ref> There is [[Japanese carpentry|not a single nail used]] in the entire structure. It takes its name from the waterfall within the complex, which runs off the nearby hills. ''Kiyomizu'' means "pure water".<ref name="PacificFriend">{{cite web|url=http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|title=Kiyomizu Temple|access-date=2008-12-18|date=2007-04-07|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090403175600/http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|archive-date=2009-04-03}}</ref><ref name="SacredDestinations" />
It was originally affiliated with the old and influential [[Hossō]] sect, dating from Nara times.<ref name=":1">Graham (2007), p. 32</ref> However, in 1965 it severed that affiliation, and its present custodians call themselves members of their own Kita-Hossō (Northern Hossō) sect.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kiyomizu-deploy |url=http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230712193733/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2023-07-12}}</ref>
=== Present ===
The expression "to jump off the stage at Kiyomizu" is the Japanese equivalent of the [[English language|English]] expression "to take the plunge".<ref name="SacredDestinations">{{cite web |title=Kiyomizudera, Kyoto |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912172631/http://www.sacred-destinations.com:80/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2009-09-12 |access-date=2008-12-18}}</ref> This refers to an [[Edo-period]] tradition that held that if one survived a {{convert|13|m|ft|adj=on|sp=us|abbr=off}} jump from the stage, one's wish would be granted. During the Edo period, 234 jumps were recorded, and of the jumpers, 85.4 per cent survived.<ref name="SacredDestinations" /> The practice was prohibited in 1872.<ref name="SacredDestinations" />
The temple complex includes several other shrines, among them the Jishu Shrine, dedicated to [[Ōkuninushi]], a god of love and "good matches".<ref name="PacificFriend" /> Jishu Shrine possesses a pair of "love stones" placed {{convert|10|m|ft|sp=us|abbr=off|-1}} apart,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jishujinja.or.jp/koiuranai/ |title=恋占いの石 |access-date=2020-08-03}}</ref> which lonely visitors can try to walk between with their eyes closed. Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>

==External links== {{Commons}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html Kiyomizu-dera Temple at Official Kyoto Travel Guide] *[http://www.kiyomizudera.or.jp Kiyomizu-dera Temple home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.taleofgenji.org/kiyomizu.html Photos and details of Kiyomizu-dera as a pilgrimage destination]

Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{For|the Buddhist temples in [[Chiba Prefecture]] and [[Shimane Prefecture]]|Seichō-ji|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kiyomizu-dera | native_name = 清水寺 | image = Kiyomizu.jpg | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kiyomizu-dera is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|42|N|135|47|06|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | sect = Kita-Hossō | deity = [[Kannon]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|www.kiyomizudera.or.jp/en}} | founded_by = [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], reconstructed by Tokugawa Iemitsu | established = 778 | year_completed = 1633 (reconstruction) }} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Kiyomizu-dera (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = "Kiyomizu-dera" in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.45 | kanji = 清水寺 | hiragana = きよみずでら | romaji = Kiyomizu-dera }}
{{Nihongo|lead=yes|'''Kiyomizu-dera'''|清水寺||{{lit|Pure Water Monastery}}}} is a [[Buddhist]] temple located in eastern [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The temple is part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/688/multiple=1&unique_number=814|title=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|access-date=2008-12-20}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Circa 1880 detail, from- Adolfo Farsari - Kiyomizu Kyoto (cropped).jpg|thumb|Kiyomizu-dera circa 1880 by [[Adolfo Farsari]]]] The temple was established in 778, during the late [[Nara period]], by [[Enchin Shonin]], who was a priest from [[Nara (city)|Nara]] (the capital of Japan from 710 to 784). He is said to have received a vision telling him to construct the temple next to the Otowa spring.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kiyomizu-dera |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Kiyomizu-dera/ |access-date=2022-07-11 |website=World History Encyclopedia |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":1" />
In 798, the [[shogun]] [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue Tamuramaro]] improved the site by including a large hall that was reassembled from the palace of [[Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kammu]] (r. 781–806). The emperor had left Nara to escape the strong influence that the Buddhist monasteries had on the government there. During this period there was a strong rivalry between the [[Kōfuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]] and the Kiyomizu-dera temples, and both had influence around the region.<ref name=":0" />
Many of the temple's present buildings were constructed in 1633 on the orders of the shogun [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]].<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=37}}</ref> There is [[Japanese carpentry|not a single nail used]] in the entire structure. It takes its name from the waterfall within the complex, which runs off the nearby hills. ''Kiyomizu'' means "pure water".<ref name="PacificFriend">{{cite web|url=http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|title=Kiyomizu Temple|access-date=2008-12-18|date=2007-04-07|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090403175600/http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|archive-date=2009-04-03}}</ref><ref name="SacredDestinations" />
It was originally affiliated with the old and influential [[Hossō]] sect, dating from Nara times.<ref name=":1">Graham (2007), p. 32</ref> However, in 1965 it severed that affiliation, and its present custodians call themselves members of their own Kita-Hossō (Northern Hossō) sect.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kiyomizu-deploy |url=http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230712193733/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2023-07-12}}</ref>
=== Present ===
The expression "to jump off the stage at Kiyomizu" is the Japanese equivalent of the [[English language|English]] expression "to take the plunge".<ref name="SacredDestinations">{{cite web |title=Kiyomizudera, Kyoto |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912172631/http://www.sacred-destinations.com:80/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2009-09-12 |access-date=2008-12-18}}</ref> This refers to an [[Edo-period]] tradition that held that if one survived a {{convert|13|m|ft|adj=on|sp=us|abbr=off}} jump from the stage, one's wish would be granted. During the Edo period, 234 jumps were recorded, and of the jumpers, 85.4 per cent survived.<ref name="SacredDestinations" /> The practice was prohibited in 1872.<ref name="SacredDestinations" />
The temple complex includes several other shrines, among them the Jishu Shrine, dedicated to [[Ōkuninushi]], a god of love and "good matches".<ref name="PacificFriend" /> Jishu Shrine possesses a pair of "love stones" placed {{convert|10|m|ft|sp=us|abbr=off|-1}} apart,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jishujinja.or.jp/koiuranai/ |title=恋占いの石 |access-date=2020-08-03}}</ref> which lonely visitors can try to walk between with their eyes closed. Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>
==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Religion|Architecture}} *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]] *[[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[Tourism in Japan]]

==External links== {{Commons}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html Kiyomizu-dera Temple at Official Kyoto Travel Guide] *[http://www.kiyomizudera.or.jp Kiyomizu-dera Temple home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.taleofgenji.org/kiyomizu.html Photos and details of Kiyomizu-dera as a pilgrimage destination]

Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{For|the Buddhist temples in [[Chiba Prefecture]] and [[Shimane Prefecture]]|Seichō-ji|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kiyomizu-dera | native_name = 清水寺 | image = Kiyomizu.jpg | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kiyomizu-dera is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|42|N|135|47|06|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | sect = Kita-Hossō | deity = [[Kannon]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|www.kiyomizudera.or.jp/en}} | founded_by = [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], reconstructed by Tokugawa Iemitsu | established = 778 | year_completed = 1633 (reconstruction) }} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Kiyomizu-dera (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = "Kiyomizu-dera" in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.45 | kanji = 清水寺 | hiragana = きよみずでら | romaji = Kiyomizu-dera }}
{{Nihongo|lead=yes|'''Kiyomizu-dera'''|清水寺||{{lit|Pure Water Monastery}}}} is a [[Buddhist]] temple located in eastern [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The temple is part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/688/multiple=1&unique_number=814|title=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|access-date=2008-12-20}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Circa 1880 detail, from- Adolfo Farsari - Kiyomizu Kyoto (cropped).jpg|thumb|Kiyomizu-dera circa 1880 by [[Adolfo Farsari]]]] The temple was established in 778, during the late [[Nara period]], by [[Enchin Shonin]], who was a priest from [[Nara (city)|Nara]] (the capital of Japan from 710 to 784). He is said to have received a vision telling him to construct the temple next to the Otowa spring.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kiyomizu-dera |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Kiyomizu-dera/ |access-date=2022-07-11 |website=World History Encyclopedia |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":1" />
In 798, the [[shogun]] [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue Tamuramaro]] improved the site by including a large hall that was reassembled from the palace of [[Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kammu]] (r. 781–806). The emperor had left Nara to escape the strong influence that the Buddhist monasteries had on the government there. During this period there was a strong rivalry between the [[Kōfuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]] and the Kiyomizu-dera temples, and both had influence around the region.<ref name=":0" />
Many of the temple's present buildings were constructed in 1633 on the orders of the shogun [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]].<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=37}}</ref> There is [[Japanese carpentry|not a single nail used]] in the entire structure. It takes its name from the waterfall within the complex, which runs off the nearby hills. ''Kiyomizu'' means "pure water".<ref name="PacificFriend">{{cite web|url=http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|title=Kiyomizu Temple|access-date=2008-12-18|date=2007-04-07|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090403175600/http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|archive-date=2009-04-03}}</ref><ref name="SacredDestinations" />
It was originally affiliated with the old and influential [[Hossō]] sect, dating from Nara times.<ref name=":1">Graham (2007), p. 32</ref> However, in 1965 it severed that affiliation, and its present custodians call themselves members of their own Kita-Hossō (Northern Hossō) sect.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kiyomizu-deploy |url=http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230712193733/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2023-07-12}}</ref>
=== Present ===
The expression "to jump off the stage at Kiyomizu" is the Japanese equivalent of the [[English language|English]] expression "to take the plunge".<ref name="SacredDestinations">{{cite web |title=Kiyomizudera, Kyoto |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912172631/http://www.sacred-destinations.com:80/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2009-09-12 |access-date=2008-12-18}}</ref> This refers to an [[Edo-period]] tradition that held that if one survived a {{convert|13|m|ft|adj=on|sp=us|abbr=off}} jump from the stage, one's wish would be granted. During the Edo period, 234 jumps were recorded, and of the jumpers, 85.4 per cent survived.<ref name="SacredDestinations" /> The practice was prohibited in 1872.<ref name="SacredDestinations" />
The temple complex includes several other shrines, among them the Jishu Shrine, dedicated to [[Ōkuninushi]], a god of love and "good matches".<ref name="PacificFriend" /> Jishu Shrine possesses a pair of "love stones" placed {{convert|10|m|ft|sp=us|abbr=off|-1}} apart,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jishujinja.or.jp/koiuranai/ |title=恋占いの石 |access-date=2020-08-03}}</ref> which lonely visitors can try to walk between with their eyes closed. Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>
==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Religion|Architecture}} *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]] *[[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[Tourism in Japan]]

==External links== {{Commons}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html Kiyomizu-dera Temple at Official Kyoto Travel Guide] *[http://www.kiyomizudera.or.jp Kiyomizu-dera Temple home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.taleofgenji.org/kiyomizu.html Photos and details of Kiyomizu-dera as a pilgrimage destination]

{{Nihongo|lead=yes|'''Kiyomizu-dera'''|清水寺||{{lit|Pure Water Monastery}}}} is a [[Buddhist]] temple located in eastern [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The temple is part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/688/multiple=1&unique_number=814|title=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|access-date=2008-12-20}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Circa 1880 detail, from- Adolfo Farsari - Kiyomizu Kyoto (cropped).jpg|thumb|Kiyomizu-dera circa 1880 by [[Adolfo Farsari]]]] The temple was established in 778, during the late [[Nara period]], by [[Enchin Shonin]], who was a priest from [[Nara (city)|Nara]] (the capital of Japan from 710 to 784). He is said to have received a vision telling him to construct the temple next to the Otowa spring.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kiyomizu-dera |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Kiyomizu-dera/ |access-date=2022-07-11 |website=World History Encyclopedia |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":1" />
In 798, the [[shogun]] [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue Tamuramaro]] improved the site by including a large hall that was reassembled from the palace of [[Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kammu]] (r. 781–806). ''Kiyomizu'' means "pure water".<ref name="PacificFriend">{{cite web|url=http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|title=Kiyomizu Temple|access-date=2008-12-18|date=2007-04-07|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090403175600/http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|archive-date=2009-04-03}}</ref><ref name="SacredDestinations" />

=== Present ===
The expression "to jump off the stage at Kiyomizu" is the Japanese equivalent of the [[English language|English]] expression "to take the plunge".<ref name="SacredDestinations">{{cite web |title=Kiyomizudera, Kyoto |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912172631/http://www.sacred-destinations.com:80/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2009-09-12 |access-date=2008-12-18}}</ref> This refers to an [[Edo-period]] tradition that held that if one survived a {{convert|13|m|ft|adj=on|sp=us|abbr=off}} jump from the stage, one's wish would be granted. During the Edo period, 234 jumps were recorded, and of the jumpers, 85.4 per cent survived.<ref name="SacredDestinations" /> The practice was prohibited in 1872.<ref name="SacredDestinations" />
The temple complex includes several other shrines, among them the Jishu Shrine, dedicated to [[Ōkuninushi]], a god of love and "good matches".<ref name="PacificFriend" /> Jishu Shrine possesses a pair of "love stones" placed {{convert|10|m|ft|sp=us|abbr=off|-1}} apart,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jishujinja.or.jp/koiuranai/ |title=恋占いの石 |access-date=2020-08-03}}</ref> which lonely visitors can try to walk between with their eyes closed. Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:1 revision imported]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{For|the Buddhist temples in [[Chiba Prefecture]] and [[Shimane Prefecture]]|Seichō-ji|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kiyomizu-dera | native_name = 清水寺 | image = Kiyomizu.jpg | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kiyomizu-dera is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|42|N|135|47|06|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | sect = Kita-Hossō | deity = [[Kannon]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|www.kiyomizudera.or.jp/en}} | founded_by = [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], reconstructed by Tokugawa Iemitsu | established = 778 | year_completed = 1633 (reconstruction) }} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Kiyomizu-dera (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = "Kiyomizu-dera" in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.45 | kanji = 清水寺 | hiragana = きよみずでら | romaji = Kiyomizu-dera }}
{{Nihongo|lead=yes|'''Kiyomizu-dera'''|清水寺||{{lit|Pure Water Monastery}}}} is a [[Buddhist]] temple located in eastern [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The temple is part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/688/multiple=1&unique_number=814|title=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|access-date=2008-12-20}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Circa 1880 detail, from- Adolfo Farsari - Kiyomizu Kyoto (cropped).jpg|thumb|Kiyomizu-dera circa 1880 by [[Adolfo Farsari]]]] The temple was established in 778, during the late [[Nara period]], by [[Enchin Shonin]], who was a priest from [[Nara (city)|Nara]] (the capital of Japan from 710 to 784). He is said to have received a vision telling him to construct the temple next to the Otowa spring.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kiyomizu-dera |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Kiyomizu-dera/ |access-date=2022-07-11 |website=World History Encyclopedia |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":1" />
In 798, the [[shogun]] [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue Tamuramaro]] improved the site by including a large hall that was reassembled from the palace of [[Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kammu]] (r. 781–806). The emperor had left Nara to escape the strong influence that the Buddhist monasteries had on the government there. During this period there was a strong rivalry between the [[Kōfuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]] and the Kiyomizu-dera temples, and both had influence around the region.<ref name=":0" />
Many of the temple's present buildings were constructed in 1633 on the orders of the shogun [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]].<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=37}}</ref> There is [[Japanese carpentry|not a single nail used]] in the entire structure. It takes its name from the waterfall within the complex, which runs off the nearby hills. ''Kiyomizu'' means "pure water".<ref name="PacificFriend">{{cite web|url=http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|title=Kiyomizu Temple|access-date=2008-12-18|date=2007-04-07|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090403175600/http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|archive-date=2009-04-03}}</ref><ref name="SacredDestinations" />
It was originally affiliated with the old and influential [[Hossō]] sect, dating from Nara times.<ref name=":1">Graham (2007), p. 32</ref> However, in 1965 it severed that affiliation, and its present custodians call themselves members of their own Kita-Hossō (Northern Hossō) sect.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kiyomizu-deploy |url=http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230712193733/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2023-07-12}}</ref>
=== Present ===
The expression "to jump off the stage at Kiyomizu" is the Japanese equivalent of the [[English language|English]] expression "to take the plunge".<ref name="SacredDestinations">{{cite web |title=Kiyomizudera, Kyoto |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912172631/http://www.sacred-destinations.com:80/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2009-09-12 |access-date=2008-12-18}}</ref> This refers to an [[Edo-period]] tradition that held that if one survived a {{convert|13|m|ft|adj=on|sp=us|abbr=off}} jump from the stage, one's wish would be granted. During the Edo period, 234 jumps were recorded, and of the jumpers, 85.4 per cent survived.<ref name="SacredDestinations" /> The practice was prohibited in 1872.<ref name="SacredDestinations" />
The temple complex includes several other shrines, among them the Jishu Shrine, dedicated to [[Ōkuninushi]], a god of love and "good matches".<ref name="PacificFriend" /> Jishu Shrine possesses a pair of "love stones" placed {{convert|10|m|ft|sp=us|abbr=off|-1}} apart,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jishujinja.or.jp/koiuranai/ |title=恋占いの石 |access-date=2020-08-03}}</ref> which lonely visitors can try to walk between with their eyes closed. Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{For|the Buddhist temples in [[Chiba Prefecture]] and [[Shimane Prefecture]]|Seichō-ji|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kiyomizu-dera | native_name = 清水寺 | image = Kiyomizu.jpg | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kiyomizu-dera is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = [[Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|42|N|135|47|06|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | sect = Kita-Hossō | deity = [[Kannon]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|www.kiyomizudera.or.jp/en}} | founded_by = [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]], reconstructed by Tokugawa Iemitsu | established = 778 | year_completed = 1633 (reconstruction) }} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Kiyomizu-dera (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = "Kiyomizu-dera" in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.45 | kanji = 清水寺 | hiragana = きよみずでら | romaji = Kiyomizu-dera }}
{{Nihongo|lead=yes|'''Kiyomizu-dera'''|清水寺||{{lit|Pure Water Monastery}}}} is a [[Buddhist]] temple located in eastern [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The temple is part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/688/multiple=1&unique_number=814|title=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|access-date=2008-12-20}}</ref>
== History == [[File:Circa 1880 detail, from- Adolfo Farsari - Kiyomizu Kyoto (cropped).jpg|thumb|Kiyomizu-dera circa 1880 by [[Adolfo Farsari]]]] The temple was established in 778, during the late [[Nara period]], by [[Enchin Shonin]], who was a priest from [[Nara (city)|Nara]] (the capital of Japan from 710 to 784). He is said to have received a vision telling him to construct the temple next to the Otowa spring.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Kiyomizu-dera |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Kiyomizu-dera/ |access-date=2022-07-11 |website=World History Encyclopedia |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":1" />
In 798, the [[shogun]] [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue Tamuramaro]] improved the site by including a large hall that was reassembled from the palace of [[Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kammu]] (r. 781–806). The emperor had left Nara to escape the strong influence that the Buddhist monasteries had on the government there. During this period there was a strong rivalry between the [[Kōfuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]] and the Kiyomizu-dera temples, and both had influence around the region.<ref name=":0" />
Many of the temple's present buildings were constructed in 1633 on the orders of the shogun [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]].<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=37}}</ref> There is [[Japanese carpentry|not a single nail used]] in the entire structure. It takes its name from the waterfall within the complex, which runs off the nearby hills. ''Kiyomizu'' means "pure water".<ref name="PacificFriend">{{cite web|url=http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|title=Kiyomizu Temple|access-date=2008-12-18|date=2007-04-07|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090403175600/http://www.pacificfriend.ca/html/kiyomizu_temple.html|archive-date=2009-04-03}}</ref><ref name="SacredDestinations" />
It was originally affiliated with the old and influential [[Hossō]] sect, dating from Nara times.<ref name=":1">Graham (2007), p. 32</ref> However, in 1965 it severed that affiliation, and its present custodians call themselves members of their own Kita-Hossō (Northern Hossō) sect.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kiyomizu-deploy |url=http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230712193733/http://kyoto.asanoxn.com/places/higashiyama_mid/kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2023-07-12}}</ref>
=== Present ===
The expression "to jump off the stage at Kiyomizu" is the Japanese equivalent of the [[English language|English]] expression "to take the plunge".<ref name="SacredDestinations">{{cite web |title=Kiyomizudera, Kyoto |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912172631/http://www.sacred-destinations.com:80/japan/kyoto-kiyomizudera.htm |archive-date=2009-09-12 |access-date=2008-12-18}}</ref> This refers to an [[Edo-period]] tradition that held that if one survived a {{convert|13|m|ft|adj=on|sp=us|abbr=off}} jump from the stage, one's wish would be granted. During the Edo period, 234 jumps were recorded, and of the jumpers, 85.4 per cent survived.<ref name="SacredDestinations" /> The practice was prohibited in 1872.<ref name="SacredDestinations" />
The temple complex includes several other shrines, among them the Jishu Shrine, dedicated to [[Ōkuninushi]], a god of love and "good matches".<ref name="PacificFriend" /> Jishu Shrine possesses a pair of "love stones" placed {{convert|10|m|ft|sp=us|abbr=off|-1}} apart,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jishujinja.or.jp/koiuranai/ |title=恋占いの石 |access-date=2020-08-03}}</ref> which lonely visitors can try to walk between with their eyes closed. Success in reaching the other stone with their eyes closed implies that the pilgrim will find true love.<ref name=".japanvisitor">{{Cite web |title=japanvisitor.com |url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715222612/https://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=357&pID=309 |archive-date=2011-07-15 |access-date=2010-08-21}}</ref>
The complex also offers various talismans, incense, and ''[[omikuji]]'' (paper fortunes). The site is particularly popular during festivals, especially at New Year's and during ''[[obon]]'' in the summer, when additional booths fill the grounds selling traditional holiday foodstuffs and souvenirs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |title=Kiyomizu-dera Temple |access-date=2008-12-20 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html |archive-date=December 10, 2008 }}</ref>
In 2007, Kiyomizu-dera was one of 21 finalists for the [[New Seven Wonders of the World]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |title=The Finalists for The Official New 7 Wonders of the World |access-date=2009-06-01 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090601172918/http://www.new7wonders.com/classic/en/n7w/finalists/ |archive-date=2009-06-01 }}</ref> but was not picked as one of the seven winning sites. The temple was covered entirely by semi-transparent scaffolding while it underwent restoration works in preparation for the [[2020 Summer Olympics|2020 Olympics]].
== Architecture == Kiyomizu-dera is located in the foothills of Mount Otowa, part of the Higashiyama mountain range that dominates eastern Kyoto. The main hall has a large veranda, supported by tall pillars, that juts out over the hillside and offers views of the city. Large verandas and main halls were constructed at many popular sites during the Edo period to accommodate large numbers of pilgrims.<ref>{{harvnb|Graham|2007|p=80}}</ref>
Beneath the main hall is the Otowa waterfall, where three channels of water fall into a pond. Visitors can catch and drink the water, which is believed to have wish-granting powers. There is also the Tainai Meguri, a dark tunnel said to represent the womb of Daizuiku Bosatsu (the bodhisattva [[Mahāpratisarā]]).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thehindu.com/life-and-style/travel/from-darkness-to-light-at-japans-kiyomizu-dera/article26720967.ece|title=From darkness to light at Japan's Kiyomizu-dera|work=The Hindu|date=3 April 2019 |accessdate=2023-02-12 |last1=Mukri |first1=Farha }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://magazine.japan-jtrip.com/article/kansai/1456/|title=Kiyomizu-dera temple is one of Kyoto's must-see attractions!|date=13 June 2016 }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -02.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera in autumn File:Kiyomizudera Snow.JPG|Kiyomizu-dera in winter File:Nio-mon, Kiyomizu-dera Temple, Kyoto, West view 20190416 1.jpg|A ''[[niōmon]]'' (''[[Deva (Buddhism)|deva]]'' gate) File:03-05-JPN074.jpg|Statuettes of [[Ksitigarbha]] (or Jizō) ''en masse'' File:Kiyomizu0800.jpg|''Otowa-no-taki'', the waterfall where visitors drink for health, longevity, and success in studies File:Kiyomizu-dera, Kyoto, November 2016 -06.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera, Illuminated File:Kiyomizu-dera 2013-10-24 (11035923746).jpg|''[[Dougong]]'' architectural element, Kiyomizu-dera File:Smok w Kiyomizudera.JPG|Dragon sculpture at Kiyomizu-dera File:Kaisan-do Kyo-do Sanju-no-to.jpg|Right to left: Kaisan-do (Founder's Hall), Kyo-do (Sutra Hall) and Sanju-no-to (Three storied Pagoda) File:Kiyomizudera-Niomon-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of Kyoto and Niōmon from Kiyomizu-dera File:Kiyomizu dera 2024.jpg|Kiyomizu-dera </gallery>
==See also== {{Portal|Japan|Religion|Architecture}} *[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]] *[[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[Tourism in Japan]]

== Sources == * {{cite book|last=Graham|first=Patricia J.|year=2007|title=Faith and Power in Japanese Buddhist Art|location=Honolulu|publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]]|isbn=978-0-8248-3126-4}} * {{cite book|author=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon|year=1956|title=Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|location=Kyoto|publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society}}
==External links== {{Commons}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20081210031004/http://www.kyoto.travel/place_to_go/kiyomizudera_temple.html Kiyomizu-dera Temple at Official Kyoto Travel Guide] *[http://www.kiyomizudera.or.jp Kiyomizu-dera Temple home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.taleofgenji.org/kiyomizu.html Photos and details of Kiyomizu-dera as a pilgrimage destination]
